title: Deaths from volcanic eruptions
subtitle: Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.
caption:
Deaths from volcanic eruptions

Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.

The bar chart records global deaths from volcanic eruptions between 1570 and 2024, with the vertical axis measuring fatalities from zero to 60,000. The data displays a pattern of long intervals with low mortality punctuated by isolated, extreme spikes. The highest visible peak occurs in 1815, reaching the 60,000 mark on the scale. A second major cluster of high mortality appears in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with bars reaching approximately 37,000 in 1883 and roughly 41,000 in 1902. Another significant event registers around 23,000 deaths in 1985. The year 1792 shows a bar reaching approximately 15,000, while 1772 sits near 10,000. Outside of these specific years, the majority of the timeline shows bars that remain very close to the zero baseline, often falling below the 5,000 level. The distribution is highly uneven, with the vast majority of recorded years showing minimal impact compared to the few catastrophic events that dominate the visual field. The data shows that while volcanic activity is continuous, the human cost concentrates in a small number of specific years where mortality exceeds tens of thousands.
